title: pathToFileUrl is called before it is defined; ReferenceError at module load in pgo-train.mjs
description: Line 14 calls `pathToFileUrl(moduleJs)` at module top-level (outside any function) but `pathToFileUrl` is defined at line 245, well below the call site. JavaScript `function` declarations hoist but this is a plain `function` — however it IS a function declaration, so hoisting applies. On second reading, hoisting saves it at runtime. BUT the issue is that lines 6-8 read env-vars and line 14 calls `import(pathToFileUrl(...))` at the top-level await without a try/catch: if `moduleJs` points to a non-existent path the dynamic import throws an uncaught top-level error with no message context. There is no try/catch around the top-level `import()` call, so failure surfaces as an opaque unhandled rejection without identifying which module path was bad.

title: Auto-staging PGO swallows all errors with bare empty catch, masking file-system or JSON parse failures
description: The auto-stage block at lines 121-129 wraps `stagePgoLock()` in an empty `catch {}`. This silently swallows any error from `stagePgoLock` including genuine failures such as JSON parse errors in a malformed pgo-manifest.json, permission errors, or disk-full conditions. Only ENOENT (file not found) is the intended silent case. All other errors should be at least warned, as they may indicate a corrupt corpus manifest that will produce incorrect PGO data.

title: cloneSubmoduleAtCommit silently ignores all errors from runGitCapture HEAD check, masking unexpected failures
description: The `try/catch {}` at lines 649-651 swallows any error from `runGitCapture` including network errors, corrupt `.git` dirs, and permission failures — the intent is to only ignore ENOENT (directory doesn't exist yet). Any real error causes silent fallthrough to a full re-clone, which may fail later with a less informative error or produce wrong results. The catch should at minimum re-throw if the error is not ENOENT/`non-zero exit`.

title: linkBridge WebAssembly.validate call does not free the invalid wasm file, and the error path does not include the reason from the validator
description: `WebAssembly.validate(wasmBytes)` at line 538 is called only on the failure branch of `WebAssembly.compile`. The thrown error at line 539 includes the compile error message but not what `validate` returned (just a boolean). More critically, `readFile(outWasm)` at line 537 may throw if the linker failed to write the file at all, propagating without a useful contextual message. The validate-only path for relaxed-simd (line 543) returns bytes without checking `_jxl_wasm_buffer_free`-style ownership — not directly a bug here but the asymmetric code path (compile vs validate-only) has no explanation for why the file isn't freed.

title: ensureLibjxlSource catch block silently swallows all errors before the rm+reclone, losing the original failure reason
description: The `try/catch` at lines 571-578 catches errors from both `stat(sourceDir)` and `access(..., .git)`. If `.git` doesn't exist the intent is to re-clone, which is correct. However if `stat()` throws an unexpected error (permission denied, I/O error) or if the `.git` check succeeds but the directory is corrupt, the catch swallows all context and silently proceeds to `rm` and re-clone. This can make debugging hard. The catch should distinguish ENOENT from other errors.

title: encodeRgb16Planar leaks WASM heap allocations when the capability check fails after partial allocation
description: In `encodeRgb16Planar` (lines 1042-1056), `ensureU16Heap` allocates rPtr, gPtr, bPtr in sequence. If the capability check at line 1045 fires, all three are freed. However `ensureU16Heap` calls `mallocOrThrow` which throws on OOM — if rPtr succeeds but gPtr throws, bPtr is never reached and rPtr is never freed. There is no try/finally to guarantee cleanup on partial allocation before the capability check.

title: encodeRgb16Planar leaks rPtr/gPtr/bPtr if takeBuffer throws after the encode call
description: After calling `m._jxl_wasm_encode_rgb16_planar(...)`, the three plane pointers are freed one by one. If `takeBuffer` at line 1055 throws (e.g. a WASM encode error), execution exits the function before the three `_free` calls. There is no try/finally wrapping the encode call and the frees. The handle itself is freed inside `takeBuffer` (in its own finally), but rPtr/gPtr/bPtr will leak.

title: parsePpmHeader in benchmark-pgo.mjs loops forever on a truncated or non-PPM input
description: The `while (tokens.length < 4)` loop at line 186 increments `i` only when it finds whitespace, a comment, or a token. If the input byte stream ends before 4 tokens are accumulated (truncated file, or the file isn't a PPM at all), `i` reaches `bytes.length` and `bytes[i]` becomes `undefined`. `isWhitespace(undefined)` returns false, `bytes[i] === 0x23` is false, the inner token loop `while (i < bytes.length && !isWhitespace(bytes[i]))` exits immediately because `i >= bytes.length`, resulting in an empty-string token that is pushed, and `i` never advances — infinite loop. pgo-train.mjs has a fix (magic-byte check) but benchmark-pgo.mjs does not.

title: eventsProgressive: inputClosed error branch throws a decode error when result===0 and inputClosed, but WASM decError may be 0 on a benign flush-complete
description: At lines 1632-1636, when `result === 0` and `inputClosed` is true, the code throws `JXL decode error: ${decError(dec)}`. But `decError` returning 0 means no error — this path fires legitimately when the decoder finishes consuming the last byte without a final result=2 (e.g. the decoder signaled DONE via the result=2 branch on the very same push that closed input). The error message `JXL decode error: 0` would be confusing. The condition should additionally check `decError(dec) !== 0`.

title: deferredRelease pre-allocation throws an Error on OOM instead of falling back, preventing decode from starting at all
description: When `deferredRelease=true`, a pre-allocation of 8 MB (1920×1080×4) is attempted at lines 1412-1419. On OOM, a hard `throw new Error(...)` stops the decoder entirely. The deferredRelease feature is optional/experimental; a better behaviour on OOM would be to fall back to the normal transfer path (set `reusablePixelBuf = null` and log a warning) rather than rejecting the entire decode session. The same issue exists in the one-shot path at lines 1741-1748.

title: preparePixelsForEmit throws synchronously inside an async generator if pixel data exceeds the deferred buffer, surfacing as an uncaught generator throw rather than a typed error event
description: `preparePixelsForEmit` at lines 1447-1461 throws a raw `Error` when `pixData.byteLength > reusablePixelCap`. This throw propagates synchronously inside `eventsProgressive` (an async generator), which means the error will be caught by the `catch (error)` at line 1363 and emitted as a `{ type: 'error', code: 'DecodeFailed', message: '...' }` event. While the error IS caught at the outer level, the message `Pixel data (...) exceeds reusable buffer capacity (...)` gives no indication that `deferredRelease` is the cause, which will be confusing. The buffer should be grown instead of throwing, since it's the facade's own pre-allocation that is too small.

title: compileFromResponse last-ditch fallback silently uses the partially-consumed response body
description: In `compileFromResponse` (lines 80-95), when `compileStreaming` fails and `getFreshResponse` is not provided (line 87 guard), the code falls through to line 94 and calls `response.arrayBuffer()` on the original response. Since `compileStreaming` already consumed part of the body (the try at line 85 reads from `response.body`), calling `arrayBuffer()` on the same response may return an empty or partial buffer — the existing comment says 'original response may be partially consumed' but still attempts it. This could silently produce a corrupt or zero-byte buffer for `WebAssembly.compile`, causing an opaque instantiation error.

title: writeIndexedDbModule is called with .catch(() => {}) but IDB open errors may propagate as unhandled rejections in some environments
description: At line 74, `writeIndexedDbModule` is called and its return promise is discarded with `.catch(() => {})`. The inline comment says 'best-effort'. However `openCacheDb` inside `writeIndexedDbModule` could throw synchronously (before the promise chain is established) in environments where `factory.open` is not a real IDBOpenDBRequest, e.g. during test teardown. While the `.catch` handles async rejections, any synchronous throw from the IDB factory call itself could bypass the `.catch` and become an unhandled rejection. This is a low-risk platform-specific edge case.

title: LibjxlEncoder.chunks() checks queuedPixelBytes vs pixelByteTotal but does not account for a race where finish() is called before any pushPixels()
description: If `finish()` is called with `queuedPixelBytes === 0` and `pixelByteTotal > 0` (i.e. no pixels were ever pushed), the check at line 2084 throws `JXL encode expected N bytes for ..., got 0`. This is correct but the streaming input path at line 2090 additionally requires `wasmEncState !== 0`. If the module loaded but `wasmEncState` was never set (cancelled mid-init), `streamingInputActive` is false but `pixelChunks` is empty too. The resulting error message `JXL encode expected N bytes for rgba8, got 0` is not obviously actionable — it should say the encoder received no pixels.

title: mergeProfiles silently succeeds if llvm-profdata is not in PATH; error only surfaces from spawn
description: `mergeProfiles` at line 429 runs `llvm-profdata merge` via `run()`. If `llvm-profdata` is not found, the error message is just `llvm-profdata exited with null/ENOENT` from the `spawn` error event. There is no upfront assertion (like `assertBinary`) to give a clear 'llvm-profdata is required for PGO' message. This is an issue in the PGO pipeline specifically because the profile data is silently missing from the pipeline if the merge step fails partway.
code_excerpt:
console.log(`[pgo] merge ${profrawFiles.length} profraw files`);
  await run("llvm-profdata", ["merge", "-output", profdataPath, ...profrawFiles], { cwd: packageRoot });

title: LibjxlEncoder uses console.log for profiling telemetry with no way to silence or route it in production
description: The encoder emits profiling data via `console.log` at line 1986 (`[jxl-wasm-enc] module+create_image setup`) and line 2298 (`[jxl-wasm-enc] facade profile`). These unconditional log lines fire on every encode including in production browser workers, polluting the console with performance numbers that are only useful during development. There is no DEBUG flag, `onMetric` hook routing for these particular lines, or structured log level. The `onMetric` callback is present for other metrics; extend it to cover the profile summary or gate behind a flag.

title: decodeTiledRegionRgba8 and decodeTileContainerRegion always console.log timing, no way to suppress in production
description: Both `decodeTiledRegionRgba8` (lines 972-977) and `decodeTileContainerRegion` (lines 1174-1179) unconditionally emit a `console.log` with region/timing details on every call. In a production viewer decoding hundreds of tiles these spam the browser console. The `onMetric` callback is already present for all the underlying numbers; route the human-readable summary through it too (or only emit when a `verbose` option is set).

title: Only CapabilityMissing has a structured error class; all other WASM errors are plain Error with no code
description: The codebase has `CapabilityMissing` with a `.code` field for missing WASM capabilities. All other error conditions — OOM, decode failure, encode failure, invalid dimensions, corrupt JXTC — use plain `new Error(...)` with no code. Consumers (jxl-worker-browser decode-handler) already match on `error instanceof CapabilityMissing`; adding sentinel error classes or at minimum `.code` strings for OOM and decode failure would allow the scheduler layer to distinguish retriable errors (transient OOM) from permanent ones (corrupt bitstream) without string-parsing.

title: perceptualConstancyApplyBulk calls WASM AVX2 function with raw JS Float32Arrays instead of WASM heap pointers
description: At line 3153, `fn(r, g, b, targetR, targetG, targetB, n, sat, vib, vibZero ? 1 : 0)` passes JavaScript `Float32Array` objects directly to a WASM exported function that expects `float*` WASM heap pointers (integer offsets). Passing JS typed arrays as WASM pointer arguments will pass the wrong values (the array's numeric properties, not a heap address). This would silently corrupt memory or produce wrong results whenever the AVX2 WASM bridge is present. The arrays need to be copied into the WASM heap with `_malloc`, pointers passed, and results copied back out.
